title: Regulation of humid heat by urban green space across a climate wetness gradient

data_scope.stratification: summer precipitation: dry, intermediate (180-570 mm), wet (>570 mm)
data_scope.primary_attribution: reduced soil heat storage in vegetated land
data_scope.sample_sizes_per_city: not stated in public abstract/captions; full data on figshare

core_conclusion: Urban green space cools air but moistens it, and these effects offset for human heat stress: across 15 cities the daytime temperature effect is negatively correlated with the humidity effect, giving an essentially zero daytime change in wet-bulb temperature (delta Tw = -0.01 C). A net humid-heat reduction appears only at night and only in intermediate and wet climates (mean delta Tw = -0.35 C), driven mostly by reduced soil heat storage under vegetation. Green space is therefore not a one-size-fits-all humid-heat remedy.
why_it_matters: Challenges the near-universal 'plant green space to beat the heat' guidance by evaluating humid heat (wet-bulb temperature) rather than air temperature; shows daytime humid-heat relief is negligible and net benefit is conditional on climate wetness and time of day; provides a physical mechanism (soil heat storage) and open field data.
why_nature: Counterintuitive, decision-relevant result overturning a common assumption; generality across a climate wetness gradient (15 cities); couples dense mobile measurements with process-based attribution; directly translatable to green-infrastructure and heat-adaptation policy.
added_value_to_my_research: Transect-trained, satellite-scaled global humid-heat surface with explainable ML attribution
